overhaul_prompt.role: You are a senior product engineer and UI designer. You are taking over a VS Code extension called "Genouk" (internally "jarvis") that reviews a developer's prompts and git diffs using an LLM, and surfaces the results in a webview panel with an animated sprite mascot. The project currently works but feels weak and "vibe coded." Your job is to make the feedback genuinely useful and the UI genuinely good. Do not ship surface-level changes. Read the code first, then act.
overhaul_prompt.prime_directive: Before writing a single line, READ THE ACTUAL CODE and make educated guesses about intent. Do not ask the user clarifying questions you could answer yourself by reading the repo. Where the code is ambiguous, infer the most likely intended behavior from naming, surrounding code, and product context, state your assumption in one line, and proceed. Bias toward action grounded in evidence over speculation.

stack.item: VS Code extension, TypeScript. Extension host code in src/, webview UI in src/webviews/jarvis-app/index.tsx (React + framer-motion, single giant file with inline styles).
LLM access is centralized in src/AIProvider.ts: Groq SDK, model "llama-3.3-70b-versatile", max_tokens 1024, single user-role message (no system role used), no temperature set.
Two reviewers call generateContent(): src/PromptReviewer.ts (rewrites a user prompt to be token-efficient, returns JSON) and src/ChangeReviewer.ts (reviews `git diff` output, returns free text).
SessionPlanner.ts and JarvisSidebarProvider.ts wire tabs ('prompts' | 'session' | 'changes' | 'audio') and post messages between webview and host.
The webview renders an animated sprite mascot ("genouk"), speech bubbles, a holographic floor ring, tabs, a prompt textarea, score bars, and token badges.

problems.problem: The reviewer feedback is shallow and generic. PromptReviewer's system prompt only says "rewrite to be concise and token-efficient" — it never reads the user's codebase, never asks what the prompt is actually trying to accomplish, and never makes educated guesses about the developer's real intent. The result is bland advice that any spellchecker could give. ChangeReviewer is similarly generic ("check for bugs, edge cases, best practices") with no project context and no severity ranking.

Neither reviewer is given any signal about the repository it is running in: no file tree, no language, no framework, no nearby code, no recent commits. The LLM is therefore forced to answer in a vacuum. Feedback should be informed by the actual project so it can make educated guesses (e.g. "this prompt targets a React component — you probably also want X").
max_tokens is 1024 and there is no system role, no temperature, and no model configurability. Strong feedback frequently needs more room and a system/user split.

The webview looks vibe coded: one 1400+ line file, hundreds of inline style objects, hardcoded hex colors mixed with VS Code theme vars, gimmicky copy ("GRID SYSTEM SECURE", "VIBE FEEDBACK ACTIVE"), inconsistent spacing/typography, and decorative effects (neon glows, floor rings) standing in for actual visual hierarchy. It reads as a demo, not a product.

goals.goal: Make prompt feedback specific, opinionated, and actionable — it should name the weakness, explain why it matters, and show the fix, not just restate the prompt.
Make both reviewers context-aware: feed them real repository signal and instruct them to make educated guesses about intent rather than answering in a vacuum.
Redesign the webview so it looks like a deliberate product: consistent design tokens, real visual hierarchy, VS Code theme integration, restrained motion.
Keep the genouk mascot and its personality — make it feel intentional, not gimmicky.

feedback_quality.item: Rewrite the PromptReviewer system prompt so it does three things: (1) infers what the user is actually trying to get the downstream LLM to do, (2) flags the specific weaknesses (ambiguity, missing constraints, missing output format, missing context, unverifiable success criteria) with a one-line "why it matters" each, and (3) returns the improved prompt PLUS a short, blunt critique. Keep the existing JSON schema (score, feedback, improvedPrompt, tokenIssues, estimatedOriginalTokens, estimatedImprovedTokens, isFinished) so the webview keeps working — strengthen the content, not the contract.
Make the critique stronger: scores should be earned and the feedback should be willing to say a prompt is bad and exactly why. Avoid hedging and praise padding.

For ChangeReviewer, instruct the model to rank findings by severity (blocker / warning / nit), tie each to a specific file/hunk in the diff, and make an educated guess about the intent of the change before critiquing it.

context_injection.item: Before calling the LLM, gather lightweight repo context and pass it in: the workspace's primary language(s), key config files (package.json name + deps), a shallow file tree, and — for prompt review — the currently open/active file's content or selection if available. Keep it within a token budget; truncate large files.
Explicitly tell the model in the prompt: "Use this repository context to make educated guesses about the developer's intent. State assumptions briefly, then give precise feedback."

In AIProvider, add a system/user message split and make model, max_tokens, and temperature configurable (settings: jarvis.model, jarvis.maxTokens). Raise the default max_tokens enough for substantive feedback.

ui_redesign.item: Extract a small design-token layer (colors, spacing scale, radii, typography, motion durations) and reference VS Code theme variables first, with sensible fallbacks. Stop scattering raw hex values through inline styles.
Establish real hierarchy: clear primary action, secondary actions, quiet metadata. The prompt input and its result should be the visual center of the 'prompts' tab.
Replace gimmick copy ("GRID SYSTEM SECURE", "VIBE FEEDBACK ACTIVE") with honest, product-grade labels. Keep genouk's personality in the speech bubbles, not in system chrome.
Tone down decorative effects: motion should reinforce state changes (loading, result-arrived, hover), not run constantly. Keep the mascot's idle float, drop effects that don't communicate anything.

Improve the result presentation: the score, token savings, and findings should be scannable at a glance with consistent badges and spacing. Make loading and error states first-class, not afterthoughts.
Where practical, break the 1400-line webview into a few focused components (Mascot, PromptTab, ChangesTab, ScoreBar, TokenBadge) without changing the message-passing contract with the host.

constraints.item: Do not break the webview<->host message contract (types like 'reviewPrompt', 'reviewChanges', 'promptReviewResult', 'changeReviewResult') unless you update both sides consistently.
Preserve the PromptReviewResult interface fields the UI already consumes.
Keep Groq as the provider; the only AIProvider changes are system/user split and configurability. Do not add new external dependencies without justification.
Respect VS Code theming — the panel must look correct in both light and dark themes.
Make educated guesses instead of asking the user; only surface a question if a decision is genuinely unrecoverable from the code.

deliverables.item: Updated AIProvider.ts with system/user split + configurable model/tokens/temp.
Updated PromptReviewer.ts and ChangeReviewer.ts with context-aware, opinionated, educated-guess prompts (same output contracts).
A context-gathering helper used by both reviewers.
A redesigned webview with a design-token layer, real hierarchy, honest copy, restrained motion, and first-class loading/error states.
A short note (3-6 bullets) listing the assumptions you made and why.

success_criteria.item: A reviewer's feedback names a concrete weakness, explains why it matters, and shows the fix — and references something real about the project.
A new engineer opening the webview reads it as a finished product, not a demo.
No regressions in the tab navigation or the prompt/diff review round-trips.
